title: Segunda División

infobox.value: [Liga Nacional de Fútbol Profesional](/wiki/Liga_Nacional_de_F%C3%BAtbol_Profesional "Liga Nacional de Fútbol Profesional")

[Tercera División](/wiki/Tercera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Tercera División") (1929–1977) [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B") (1977–2021) [Primera Federación](/wiki/Primera_Federaci%C3%B3n "Primera Federación") (2021–present)
[LaLiga TV Hypermotion](/wiki/Liga_Nacional_de_F%C3%BAtbol_Profesional "Liga Nacional de Fútbol Profesional") [#Vamos por Movistar Plus+](/wiki/Movistar_Plus%2B "Movistar Plus+")

or **La Liga 2**, and officially known as **LaLiga HyperMotion**[[b]](#cite_note-2) for sponsorship reasons, is the men's second professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") division of the [Spanish football league system](/wiki/Spanish_football_league_system "Spanish football league system"). Administered by [Liga Nacional de Fútbol Profesional](/wiki/Liga_Nacional_de_F%C3%BAtbol_Profesional "Liga Nacional de Fútbol Profesional"), it is contested by 22 teams, with the top two teams plus the winner of a [play-off](/wiki/La_Liga_play-offs "La Liga play-offs") [promoted](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") to [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") and replaced by the three lowest-placed teams in that division.

The **Campeonato Nacional de Liga de Segunda División**,[[a]](#cite_note-1) commonly known as **Segunda División** or **La Liga 2**, and officially known as **LaLiga HyperMotion**[[b]](#cite_note-2) for sponsorship reasons, is the men's second professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") division of the [Spanish football league system](/wiki/Spanish_football_league_system "Spanish football league system"). Administered by [Liga Nacional de Fútbol Profesional](/wiki/Liga_Nacional_de_F%C3%BAtbol_Profesional "Liga Nacional de Fútbol Profesional"), it is contested by 22 teams, with the top two teams plus the winner of a [play-off](/wiki/La_Liga_play-offs "La Liga play-offs") [promoted](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") to [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") and replaced by the three lowest-placed teams in that division.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Second Division National Championship was inaugurated concurrently with the [First Division](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"), during the 1928-29 season. This setup comprised twenty teams divided into two groups: A and B. Group A functioned as the secondary national level, where the leading team would contest promotion to the First Division and the bottom two faced relegation to the Third Division. Conversely, Group B represented the third tier, wherein two teams were promoted to the Second Division, while the remaining eight joined the newly formed Third Division in the subsequent season.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For this inaugural season, Group A consisted of the following clubs: [Sevilla F. C.](/wiki/Sevilla_Football_Club "Sevilla Football Club"), [Iberia S. C.](/wiki/Real_Zaragoza "Real Zaragoza"), [Deportivo Alavés](/wiki/Deportivo_Alav%C3%A9s "Deportivo Alavés"), [Real Sporting de Gijón](/wiki/Real_Sporting_de_Gij%C3%B3n "Real Sporting de Gijón"), [Valencia F. C.](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF"), [Real Betis Balompié](/wiki/Real_Betis_Balompi%C3%A9 "Real Betis Balompié"), [Real Oviedo F. C.](/wiki/Real_Oviedo "Real Oviedo"), [Real Club Celta](/wiki/Real_Club_Celta_de_Vigo "Real Club Celta de Vigo"), [R. C. Deportivo de La Coruña](/wiki/Real_Club_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a "Real Club Deportivo de La Coruña"), and [Racing Club de Madrid](/wiki/Racing_Club_de_Madrid "Racing Club de Madrid"). On the other hand, Group B featured [Cultural y Deportiva Leonesa](/wiki/Cultural_y_Deportiva_Leonesa "Cultural y Deportiva Leonesa"), [Real Murcia F. C.](/wiki/Real_Murcia_C.F. "Real Murcia C.F."), [C. D. Castellón](/wiki/Club_Deportivo_Castell%C3%B3n "Club Deportivo Castellón"), [C. D. Torrelavega](/wiki/Real_Sociedad_Gimn%C3%A1stica_de_Torrelavega "Real Sociedad Gimnástica de Torrelavega"), [Zaragoza C. D.](/wiki/Real_Zaragoza "Real Zaragoza"), [Real Valladolid Deportivo](/wiki/Real_Valladolid_Deportivo "Real Valladolid Deportivo"), [C. A. Osasuna](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_Osasuna "Club Atlético Osasuna"), Tolosa F. C., Barakaldo F. C., and Cartagena F. C.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The structure and number of teams in the competition have evolved over time. In the 1934-35 season, the league was segmented into multiple groups. This format persisted until the 1968-69 season when it transitioned back to the singular group system that is in place today. From 1977 to 1984, when its management transitioned to the National Professional Football League, the tournament was referred to as Second Division A, after the introduction of the Second Division B as the third level in the national football hierarchy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During the 2019-20 season, a global outbreak of [severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2 "SARS-CoV-2") emerged,[[1]](#cite_note-3) which had originated in Asia and spread to Europe.[[2]](#cite_note-4)[[3]](#cite_note-5) As the virus rapidly spread across the continent, leading to rising infections and fatalities, sports entities began implementing preventative measures. In Spain, to mitigate the spread, only one match was held behind closed doors, without spectators,[[4]](#cite_note-6) yet the concern and rate of infections did not diminish, with several players and club executives testing positive. In light of the escalating situation, [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") opted to halt all competitions temporarily,[[5]](#cite_note-7) following a precedent set by [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"), which had suspended both the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") and the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League").[[6]](#cite_note-8)[[7]](#cite_note-9) In a similar vein, Italy's [CONI](/wiki/Italian_National_Olympic_Committee "Italian National Olympic Committee") and [FIGC](/wiki/Italian_Football_Federation "Italian Football Federation") put the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") on hold due to the same health concerns.[[8]](#cite_note-10) After a period of lockdown which saw a decrease in the spread of the virus, the government allowed sporting competitions to recommence,[[9]](#cite_note-11) culminating on July 20 as the remaining games were played, mirroring events in the First Division. Nonetheless, on the final matchday, multiple players from [Club de Fútbol Fuenlabrada, S.A.D.](/wiki/CF_Fuenlabrada "CF Fuenlabrada") were diagnosed with the virus. Consequently, their pivotal game against [Real Club Deportivo de La Coruña](/wiki/Real_Club_Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a "Real Club Deportivo de La Coruña"), which was of great importance to the league standings, was delayed. This disruption impacted several clubs and the ensuing promotion playoffs.[[10]](#cite_note-12)

### Naming Conventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Naming Conventions")]

The 2006-07 and 2007-08 seasons marked the first instances when the championship adopted a commercial designation, being named "Liga [BBVA](/wiki/BBVA "BBVA")" following a sponsorship agreement between the National Professional Football League and the [bank](/wiki/Banco_Bilbao_Vizcaya_Argentaria "Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria") of the same title.[[11]](#cite_note-13) From the 2008-09 through to the 2015-16 seasons, the division was rebranded as "Liga Adelante" as the bank transitioned to sponsor the First Division.[[12]](#cite_note-14) In the 2016-17 season, [Banco Santander](/wiki/Banco_Santander "Banco Santander") emerged as the primary sponsor, prompting the names "LaLiga 1|2|3" (with an enlarged "2" thus taking on the "LaLiga 2" moniker unsponsored). From the 2019-20 season onward, it became "LaLiga SmartBank". During the 2023-24 season, the new sponsor was introduced as [EA Sports](/wiki/EA_Sports "EA Sports"), resulting in the title "LaLiga Hypermotion".[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16)

### Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Records")]

Real Murcia has participated in the Second Division for the most seasons, a total of 53, and has secured the championship title on eight occasions. They are followed by [Sporting de Gijón](/wiki/Real_Sporting_de_Gij%C3%B3n "Real Sporting de Gijón") with 52 seasons, [Tenerife](/wiki/Club_Deportivo_Tenerife "Club Deportivo Tenerife") 48, [Sabadell](/wiki/Sabadell "Sabadell") 44, [Hércules CF](/wiki/H%C3%A9rcules_CF "Hércules CF"), [Levante UD](/wiki/Levante_UD "Levante UD") 43, [Deportivo de La Coruña](/wiki/Deportivo_de_La_Coru%C3%B1a "Deportivo de La Coruña"), [Real Oviedo](/wiki/Real_Oviedo "Real Oviedo"), [CD Castellón](/wiki/CD_Castell%C3%B3n "CD Castellón") and [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz_Club_de_F%C3%BAtbol "Cádiz Club de Fútbol") each with 42 seasons.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sociedad Deportiva Eibar holds the record for consecutive seasons in the division, with 18 seasons running from 1987/88 to 2005/06.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Among all teams that have competed in this division, only six have never featured in lower divisions: [Atlético de Madrid](/wiki/Club_Atl%C3%A9tico_de_Madrid "Club Atlético de Madrid"), [Espanyol](/wiki/Real_Club_Deportivo_Espanyol "Real Club Deportivo Espanyol"), [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_Club_de_F%C3%BAtbol "Valencia Club de Fútbol"), [Sevilla](/wiki/Sevilla_F%C3%BAtbol_Club "Sevilla Fútbol Club"), [Real Sociedad](/wiki/Real_Sociedad_de_F%C3%BAtbol "Real Sociedad de Fútbol"), and [Sporting de Gijón](/wiki/Real_Sporting_de_Gij%C3%B3n "Real Sporting de Gijón").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 2011-12 season, Deportivo de La Coruña set a new record by amassing 91 points, leading them to clinch the championship.[[15]](#cite_note-17)[[16]](#cite_note-18) The subsequent season, 2012–13, witnessed [Elche](/wiki/Elche_CF "Elche CF") as the first team to maintain the top position throughout all 42 matchdays.[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20)

## League format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: League format")]

The league contains 22 teams that play each other home and away for a 42-match season. Each year three teams are promoted to La Liga. The top two teams earn an automatic promotion. The third team to be promoted is the winner of a play-off between the teams that finished 3rd to 6th ([reserve teams](/wiki/Reserve_team "Reserve team") are not eligible for promotion). The play-offs comprise two-legged semi-finals followed by a two-legged final. The bottom four are relegated to [Primera Federación](/wiki/Primera_Federaci%C3%B3n "Primera Federación").[[19]](#cite_note-21)
